planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun for the darkest leaf color.

directions: Grow in full sun and well-drained soil to keep the deep purple-black foliage from greening. Sturdy stems hold the color all season and are topped by dusky pink flower heads in late summer. Cut back hard in early spring to encourage compact regrowth.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and gritty soil.

directions: This low, spreading selection has small blue-gray leaves flushed purple on trailing stems. Grow in sharply drained soil and full sun, watering sparingly once established. Rich rose-pink flower clusters open in early fall.

planting.note: Plant out after frost; treat as annual in cold zones.

directions: This bright selection forms mounds of fine, needle-like chartreuse-yellow foliage that glows in sun. Grow in well-drained soil with full sun to light shade and water sparingly. Yellow star flowers appear in late spring; in cold regions grow it as a container annual.

planting.note: Set out after frost when nights stay warm.

directions: This pink-and-cream sport of the jelly bean sedum has plump bean-shaped leaves that deepen to rose in bright sun. Grow in gritty soil with strong light and water only when dry, as the leaves detach easily. Yellow star flowers appear in spring.

planting.note: Plant out after frost; it tolerates brief light freezes.

directions: This species forms loose blue-green rosettes on trailing stems that spill over pot edges and walls. Grow in well-drained soil and full sun, watering only when dry. Showers of bright yellow-orange flowers open at the stem tips in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in warm spring weather for quick establishment.

directions: Push a rooted stem section an inch into gritty soil and firm it lightly. Bright light brings out the reddish-bronze tint and tightens the toothed, stacked leaves. Give it very little water in winter and expect the occasional orange bloom on mature stems.

planting.note: Establish young branched plants in spring so roots settle before winter.

directions: Give this branching, tree-like aloe deep sharp-draining soil and room to spread its flattened fans of blue-green leaves. Water moderately through the cool growing season and keep it dry in summer dormancy. Scarlet tubular flowers appear on mature plants in late winter to spring.

planting.note: Grow from seed under warmth, then plant out young trees in warm weather.

directions: Sow fresh seed on warm, gritty soil and keep barely moist until sprouts appear. Site the slow-growing tree aloe in full sun with excellent drainage and protect it from frost and standing water. After many years mature specimens send up bright yellow flower panicles in winter.

planting.note: Transplant seedlings out after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Set this large single-stemmed aloe in full sun with fast-draining soil and give it plenty of room. Its spiny gray-green leaves form a broad rosette atop a stout trunk over the years. Tall branched racemes of orange to yellow flowers open in winter, drawing sunbirds and bees.

planting.note: Divide and replant offsets in spring as growth resumes.

directions: Plant offsets in gritty soil where the clumping rosettes can spread freely. Strong sun turns the leaf margins and their golden teeth a warm orange-red. Water sparingly, and enjoy the orange flower spikes that rise in summer above established clumps.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring near a support they can scramble through.

directions: Set rooted cuttings beside a shrub or trellis so the slender stems can lean and climb. It grows fast with light watering and tolerates more moisture than most aloes. Bright orange-red flowers appear over a long season, especially in warm months.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in full sun for the best leaf color.

directions: Give this clumping aloe full sun and lean soil so its long, recurving leaves flush coppery red under stress. Water deeply but infrequently and keep it dry through winter. Branched spikes of yellow to orange flowers rise in the cool season.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring where they get full afternoon sun.

directions: Plant this suckering aloe in full sun and hold back on water to intensify its deep copper-red foliage. It clumps steadily and shrugs off drought once established. Rich orange-red flowers open on branched spikes in fall and early winter.

planting.note: Transplant seedlings out once warm weather is settled.

directions: Grow this stemless aloe in rocky, sharply drained soil in full sun. The blue-green rosette bears spotted, toothed leaves and stores water well through drought. Striking two-toned flower spikes, dark red in bud and opening yellow, appear in winter.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring; this is one of the cold-toughest aloes.

directions: Set rooted cuttings in well-drained soil where the shrubby stems can bush out. Unusually cold-hardy for an aloe, it tolerates light frost when kept dry in winter. Yellow flower spikes rise above the striated foliage in summer and fall.

planting.note: Divide offsets in spring; ideal as a bright windowsill succulent.

directions: Plant this small Madagascan aloe in a gritty mix in bright, filtered light. Its pale-speckled leaves take on a pink blush under stronger sun, and it clusters into a tidy colony. Coral-orange flower spikes appear on mature rosettes, and offsets root easily.

planting.note: Lift and replant offsets in spring for strongest color.

directions: Grow this festive hybrid in bright sun to bring out the red bumps and margins against green leaves. Plant in fast-draining soil and water only when dry. Cold nights and strong light deepen the holiday coloring, and it offsets readily into a small clump.

planting.note: Separate the freely produced offsets in spring.

directions: Set this compact hybrid in a gritty mix in bright light where its white-speckled dark leaves stay well marked. It multiplies quickly into a dense cluster of small rosettes. Water sparingly, and expect slender coral flower spikes on established plants.

planting.note: Divide offsets in spring for a fuller container display.

directions: Plant this popular hybrid in full sun so its speckled leaves flush warm pink at the margins. Use a sharp-draining mix and let it dry fully between waterings. It clumps steadily and sends up soft coral flower spikes over a long season.

planting.note: Separate offsets in spring and pot in a gritty succulent mix.

directions: Grow this rough-textured gasteria in bright, indirect light and a very well-drained mix. Its dark, warty tongue-shaped leaves form tight rosettes that slowly offset. Water lightly, avoid wetting the crown, and watch for arching sprays of curved pink-orange flowers in spring.

planting.note: Detach clustered offsets in spring for easy propagation.

directions: This dwarf gasteria makes plump gray-green leaves and clumps densely in a shallow, gritty pot. Keep it in bright shade to light sun and water only when the soil is dry. Its compact size suits windowsills, and slender coral flower stems appear on mature clusters.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring and let cut surfaces dry before potting.

directions: Plant this variable species in a fast-draining mix in bright, filtered light. The thick keeled leaves are flecked with pale spots and form spreading rosettes. Water sparingly year-round and enjoy the curved, stomach-shaped pink flowers on tall stems in spring.

planting.note: Remove offsets in spring for the easiest increase.

directions: Grow this slow, fan-leaved gasteria in bright shade and a sharply drained mix. The tongue-like leaves stay in two ranks when young before forming a rosette. Water lightly, protect from frost, and look for tall arching sprays of pink flowers in late winter.

planting.note: Divide mature clumps in spring; this is among the largest gasterias.

directions: Give this robust species room, as its sword-shaped leaves form a large spreading rosette. Grow it in bright light with excellent drainage and water moderately in the growing season. Tall branched spikes of reddish flowers rise well above the foliage in spring.

planting.note: Separate offsets in spring; this hybrid clusters readily.

directions: This aloe-and-gasteria cross forms tidy rosettes of frosty, white-marked green leaves. Grow it in bright, indirect light with a gritty mix and let it dry between waterings. It offsets freely into a full clump and sends up slender coral flower spikes.

planting.note: Remove offsets in spring and pot in a fast-draining mix.

directions: Grow this stemless haworthia in bright shade so the translucent leaf windows stay clear. Its retused, flattened leaf tips spread into a low star-shaped rosette. Water only when the mix dries, and expect thin spikes of small white flowers in the growing season.

planting.note: Detach offsets or root stem sections in spring.

directions: This columnar haworthia stacks its tubercled leaves along an upright stem that reddens in bright light. Grow it in gritty soil with good airflow and water sparingly. It clusters into a colony over time and produces small white tubular flowers on wiry stalks.

planting.note: Divide the clustering stems in spring.

directions: Grow this upright haworthia for its slim columns of dark leaves banded with raised white tubercles. Bright light keeps the stems compact and can flush them reddish. Water lightly, let it dry between drinks, and it will build up a dense cluster of stems.

planting.note: Separate offsets in spring and keep out of harsh sun.

directions: This soft, lacy haworthia forms rosettes edged with fine translucent bristles that give it a cobwebbed look. Grow it in bright shade with a very well-drained mix and water carefully to avoid rot. It clusters slowly and lifts thin spikes of small white flowers.

planting.note: Remove offsets in spring; grows well in a bright window.

directions: Plant this compact haworthia in bright, filtered light so the windowed leaf tips catch the sun. The rosette often blushes purple-brown under stronger light and stays small and tidy. Water only when dry, and thin flower stalks with tiny white blooms appear in the warm season.

planting.note: Divide the freely offsetting clumps in spring.

directions: This clustering haworthia makes rosettes of plump, translucent-tipped leaves that turn reddish in bright light. It offsets prolifically and quickly fills a shallow pot. Grow it in bright shade with gritty soil and water only after the mix has dried.

planting.note: Remove offsets in spring; a prized collector's rosette.

directions: Grow this handsome retuse-leaved haworthia in bright shade to show off the netted patterning on its windowed leaf faces. Keep it in a fast-draining mix and water sparingly, especially in summer. It stays compact and produces slender spikes of small white flowers.

planting.note: Propagate by root cuttings or offsets in spring.

directions: This window-leaf haworthia has flat-topped leaves that look neatly sliced across, each with a translucent window. Grow it in bright, indirect light and a very gritty mix, keeping it on the dry side. It is slow and treasured by collectors, offsetting only occasionally.

planting.note: Divide clustering stems in spring.

directions: Grow this upright haworthia for its powdery blue-gray leaves stacked into short columns. It clusters at the base to form a colony over time. Give it bright light with excellent drainage, water lightly, and keep it dry through winter.

planting.note: Detach basal offsets in spring for easy increase.

directions: This distinctive haworthia stacks its triangular leaves in three neat vertical ranks along the stem. Bright light keeps the columns tight and can bronze the foliage. Grow it in a gritty mix, water only when dry, and let it build into a clump of stems.

planting.note: Plant out young rosettes in spring in sharply drained soil.

directions: Site this broad-leaved agave in full sun with excellent drainage and room to reach its full spread. The wide, cupped powder-blue leaves make a bold symmetrical rosette that tolerates cold better than most agaves. After many years the plant sends up a towering flower stalk and then dies, leaving offsets.

planting.note: Set out young plants after frost; grow from seed as it rarely offsets.

directions: Grow this graceful agave for its dense globe of thin, dark green leaves fringed with white curling threads. Give it full sun to light shade and fast-draining soil, watering sparingly. It seldom pups, so raise new plants from seed, and expect flowering only after a decade or more.

planting.note: Plant offsets or young rosettes in spring in gritty soil.

directions: This spineless agave arches its narrow green leaves outward like tentacles, softening rock gardens and containers. Grow it in full sun to part shade with sharp drainage and little water. Unusually, it can flower and survive rather than dying, and it slowly offsets into a small colony.

planting.note: Transplant young rosettes out once nights are warm.

directions: Grow this compact agave for its blue-gray leaves with wavy, toothed margins and dark terminal spines. Give it full sun and excellent drainage, and let the soil dry fully between waterings. It stays a manageable size and eventually throws a tall branched flower stalk before setting bulbils.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in full sun and lean soil.

directions: This tidy agave forms a rosette of narrow gray-blue leaves each tipped with a striking black spine. Grow it in full sun with fast drainage and minimal water for the best color and form. It offsets modestly, and mature plants send up a tall flower spike before dying.

planting.note: One of the most cold-hardy agaves; plant out in spring with sharp drainage.

directions: Grow this small, cold-tough agave in extremely well-drained, gravelly soil in full sun. Its rigid gray-green leaves carry long terminal spines and form compact rosettes. Keep it very dry in winter to prevent rot, and expect a tall slender flower spike only after many years.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring; allow generous room for its large spread.

directions: Give this large, nearly smooth-edged agave plenty of space in full sun and well-drained soil. Its broad gray-green leaves form a graceful, mostly spineless rosette. Water deeply but rarely, and after years it produces a massive branched flower stalk and abundant offsets.

planting.note: Plant out in spring in full sun; tolerant of cold high-desert winters.

directions: Grow this desert agave in full sun with gritty, fast-draining soil and minimal supplemental water. The gray-green leaves form an open rosette and the plant is hardy to real cold when kept dry. Its tall night-blooming flower stalk feeds bats and pollinators before the rosette dies.

planting.note: A very cold-hardy agave; plant out in spring with excellent drainage.

directions: Site this compact, cold-hardy agave in full sun with sharp drainage in rock or gravel gardens. Its broad silvery-blue leaves carry stout marginal teeth and a strong terminal spine. Keep it dry in winter, and after a decade or more it lifts a tall flowering stalk.

planting.note: Set out nursery plants in spring; this solitary hybrid rarely offsets.

directions: Plant this striking hybrid where its blue-green leaves show off their glowing red-and-yellow margins backlit by the sun. Give it full sun and very well-drained soil, watering only when dry. It stays solitary and slow, making a clean architectural accent in beds or pots.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring; this hybrid clumps into a colony.

directions: This soft-leaved hybrid arches its blue-green leaves upward like flames and forms suckering clumps over time. Grow it in full sun to light shade with fast-draining soil and modest water. Its gentle, nearly toothless leaves make it a friendly choice for pathways and containers.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring; this Manfreda-Agave cross clumps well.

directions: This vigorous mangave forms a large rosette of broad gray-green leaves heavily freckled with maroon spots that darken in sun. Grow it in full sun with good drainage and light water. It offsets to form a clump and, unlike true agaves, can bloom without dying.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in a bright, well-drained spot.

directions: Grow this compact mangave for its narrow gray-green leaves marked with dark red spotting that intensifies in strong light. Give it full sun and fast-draining soil, watering sparingly. It stays small and tidy, offsets slowly, and makes a colorful container succulent.

planting.note: Set out offsets in spring for the best leaf coloring.

directions: This mangave forms a smooth rosette of wide leaves washed in dusty lavender-purple tones that deepen under bright sun. Grow it in full sun with sharp drainage and go easy on water. It makes an elegant, low mound that offsets modestly and can flower without dying.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings once nights stay reliably above 50 F.

directions: Lay or root a stem cutting in fast-draining cactus mix, burying one node just below the surface. Let the arching branches spill over a wall or hanging pot and give bright, filtered light for best leaf color. Water only when the top inch dries, and expect clusters of small white-and-yellow star flowers in autumn.

planting.note: One of the cold-hardier crassulas, tolerating brief dips near 20 F in dry soil.

directions: Root short cuttings in gritty soil and let the woody, branching trunk develop its natural bonsai-like form. Grow in full sun with sharp drainage and keep it dry through winter cold. Masses of tiny pink-tinged white flowers cover the shrub in late summer.

planting.note: A shy grower best kept in a pot that can move indoors for winter.

directions: Grow this miniature in a very lean, mineral mix with excellent drainage and bright but not scorching light. Its tightly stacked square columns of leaves resent overwatering, so water sparingly and let it dry fully between drinks. Small honey-scented flowers appear from the column tips in cooler months.

planting.note: Slow biennial-to-perennial that dies back after its striking bloom.

directions: Sow the fine seed on a gritty surface and grow the tight leaf columns hard in full sun with minimal water. Keep it nearly dry in summer dormancy to prevent rot at the base. In spring a dense dome of fragrant flowers erupts from the top, after which offsets carry the plant on.

planting.note: Winter-growing species that rests in the heat of summer.

directions: Plant in a deep, gritty mix and give bright light with careful, minimal watering. The overlapping gray scaly leaves that mimic a snake's tail rot easily if kept wet, so water only during its cool-season growth. Cream to greenish flowers tuck into the column tip in winter.

planting.note: A slow, compact grower ideal for shallow display pots.

directions: Grow the chalky white-dusted leaf columns in a lean mineral mix with strong light to keep them tight. Water sparingly and never leave the crown damp, as its dense form traps moisture. Slender flower stalks bearing tiny cream blooms rise from the tips in cooler weather.

planting.note: A free-flowering hybrid that blooms readily in containers.

directions: Root leaf or stem cuttings in fast-draining mix and give bright light to encourage bloom. Keep the compact rosettes on the dry side, watering only when the soil dries out. Dense heads of soft pink flowers form on short stems in late winter and spring.

planting.note: A dwarf hybrid prized for showy flower heads on a tiny plant.

directions: Propagate from offsets or leaves set in gritty soil and grow in bright light for tight, powdery gray rosettes. Water carefully from the side to avoid wetting the crowded foliage. Rounded clusters of fragrant pink flowers cap the plant in winter and early spring.

planting.note: An easy trailing-to-mounding species covered in soft white hairs.

directions: Root stem tips easily in cactus mix and grow in bright light to keep the hairy green leaves dense. Water when the soil dries and avoid soggy conditions that mat the fuzz. Small white star flowers appear at the branch ends in autumn and winter.

planting.note: A forest-floor species that prefers more shade and humidity than most crassulas.

directions: Root cuttings in a well-drained but slightly richer mix and shelter from harsh sun to protect the glossy dark leaves with pale undersides. Keep evenly moist during growth but never waterlogged. Tall stems of white tubular flowers rise above the foliage in autumn.

planting.note: A dwarf jade cultivar with smaller, redder leaves than the species.

directions: Root a stem or leaf cutting in gritty soil and grow in strong light to bring out the red leaf edges. Water only when the mix dries fully, easing off in winter. Mature plants may produce clusters of small white-pink star flowers in the cool season.

planting.note: Grown for its wavy, twisting blue-gray leaves on an upright shrub.

directions: Root a woody cutting in cacty mix and site in full sun to deepen the purple leaf margins. Water deeply but infrequently and keep dry through winter. Established shrubs bear clusters of pale pink-white star flowers in late winter.

planting.note: A compact branching shrublet with red-edged paired leaves.

directions: Root stem cuttings in fast-draining soil and grow in full sun to intensify the crimson leaf edges. Water sparingly and let it dry between waterings to keep the habit tight. Small pale flowers appear in clusters during the cooler months.

planting.note: A mat-forming miniature that clumps into a dense green cushion.

directions: Divide clumps or press small rosettes into gritty soil to build a tight ground-hugging mat. Give bright light and light, regular watering during growth while avoiding standing water. Short stalks of tiny white flowers cover the cushion in late winter and spring.

planting.note: A low, spreading Kalanchoe with chalky-white dusted leaves.

directions: Root stem tips in cactus mix and grow in bright light to keep the silvery bloom on the leaves. Water at the base to preserve the powdery coating and let the soil dry between waterings. Sprays of pink flowers arch out over the foliage in late winter and early spring.

planting.note: A trailing species grown for pendant bell-shaped flowers, good in baskets.

directions: Root cuttings in fast-draining mix and let the stems cascade from a hanging pot. Give bright light and water when the top inch dries, reducing water after bloom. Nodding coral-red bells dangle from the branch tips in late winter and spring.

planting.note: A small shrub with fan-shaped silver leaves marked in brown.

directions: Lay leaves or root short cuttings in gritty soil to start new plants. Grow in bright, filtered light and water sparingly, since the wedge-shaped leaves drop if overwatered. Tiny greenish flowers striped with red appear on slender stems in spring.

planting.note: A soft-fuzzy shrublet with scalloped, felted gray-green leaves.

directions: Root stem cuttings in cactus mix and grow in bright light for compact, velvety growth. Water at the soil line and allow it to dry between waterings to protect the fuzzy leaves. Greenish-yellow flowers appear on upright stems in spring.

planting.note: Produces walking-stem runners that root new plantlets at their tips.

directions: Pin down the plantlets carried on its long runners to start new rosettes, or root a leaf. Grow in bright light with careful watering, letting the broad red-edged leaves dry between drinks. Upright stems bear pink to white flowers, after which the parent runners spread further.

planting.note: A silver-leaved shrub that can grow tall in frost-free gardens.

directions: Root cuttings in gritty soil and grow in full sun to keep the silvery, spoon-shaped leaves bright. Water deeply but infrequently, tapering off in the cool season. Clusters of orange-red flowers top the branches in winter.

planting.note: Grown for green leaves boldly mottled with maroon banding.

directions: Root cuttings in fast-draining soil and give bright light to sharpen the purple leaf markings. Water when the top of the mix dries and avoid soggy roots. A tall stalk of small purple-tinged flowers rises above the rosette in summer.

planting.note: A low creeper densely coated in white woolly hairs.

directions: Root stem cuttings in gritty mix and grow in bright light to keep the felted white coating dense. Water carefully at the base and let the soil dry, as the woolly leaves hold moisture. Pink flowers appear on short stems in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Large paddle leaves grow plantlets along their edges and tips.

directions: Detach the plantlets that form at the leaf tips and set them on gritty soil to root. Grow in bright light and water when the mix dries, easing off in winter. A tall stem of large greenish tubular bells appears before the plant declines and its offsets take over.

planting.note: Upright stems carry cupped, shovel-shaped blue-green leaves.

directions: Root stem cuttings in fast-draining soil and grow in full sun for compact, upright form and the best waxy blue color. Water deeply but let the soil dry fully between waterings. Clusters of orange-red flowers form atop the stems in winter.

planting.note: Broad rounded leaves flush deep red at the edges in bright light.

directions: Root offsets or cuttings in gritty soil and grow in full sun to bright light to bring out the red-blushed leaf margins. Water when the soil dries and reduce water in winter. A tall powdery flower spike of pale yellow blooms rises at maturity, after which that rosette sets offsets and dies.

planting.note: Leaves turn brilliant red under sun and drought stress.

directions: Root cuttings in cactus mix and grow in full sun with lean watering to trigger the deep red leaf color. Water only when the soil is dry, keeping it especially dry in winter. Clusters of yellow flowers top the stems in the cool season.

planting.note: A sterile hybrid whose pink plantlets cannot survive alone, so it is not weedy.

directions: Propagate only from stem cuttings, since the showy pink edge plantlets lack chlorophyll and will not root. Grow in bright light and water when the soil dries. Rows of bright pink bracts and baby plantlets line the leaf margins, giving a butterfly-like display.

planting.note: A branching hybrid with rosettes shading from cream to green to red edges.

directions: Root a rosette-tipped cutting in fast-draining mix and grow in bright, filtered light for the strongest tricolor blend. Water during the cool growing season and keep drier in summer dormancy. Pale yellow flowers may appear on a mature rosette, which then dies while side branches carry on.

planting.note: An upright cultivar with wine-purple rosettes on branching stems.

directions: Root a stem cutting in gritty soil and grow in full sun to deepen the purple leaf color. Water in the cool growing months and reduce water sharply in summer rest. Large cones of yellow flowers rise from mature rosettes, which die back afterward.

planting.note: Forms very large, nearly stemless velvety rosettes.

directions: Root offsets or grow from seed in gritty mix and give bright, filtered light. Water through the cool season and keep the broad fuzzy rosette drier during summer dormancy. A tall dome of yellow-green flowers appears after several years, ending that rosette.

planting.note: A large solitary rosette that rarely offsets.

directions: Grow from seed or a rare offset in gritty, well-drained soil with bright light. Water during cool growth and keep on the dry side in summer. A broad, flat rust-red flower head forms after many years, after which the single rosette dies.

planting.note: A low clumping species forming tight green rosettes.

directions: Divide clumps or root offsets in fast-draining soil and grow in bright light. Water during the cool season and reduce water in summer heat. Bright yellow flowers rise on short stems, and the clumping habit fills in quickly.

planting.note: A tiny branching shrub with sticky, red-striped green leaves.

directions: Root short cuttings in gritty soil and grow in bright light to bring out the red leaf stripes. Water in the cool growing season and keep nearly dry when it rests in summer. Small yellow flowers appear on the branch tips in spring.

planting.note: A tall single-stemmed species with a wide flat rosette.

directions: Root a stem cutting or grow from seed in gritty soil with full sun to bright light. Water during the cool season and keep drier in summer. After several years a very large pink-white flower spike forms, and the tall rosette dies once it blooms.

planting.note: A vivid hybrid with rosettes streaked green, red, and yellow.

directions: Root a rosette cutting in fast-draining soil and grow in bright light to intensify the multicolored variegation. Water in the cool season and keep drier through summer dormancy. Yellow flowers may top a mature rosette, which dies as the branches continue.

planting.note: A freely branching shrub with small red-edged rosettes.

directions: Root stem cuttings in gritty soil and grow in bright light to sharpen the coppery red leaf edges. Water in cool weather and reduce water during summer rest. Pale pink to white flowers appear on mature rosettes, which then die back while the shrub carries on.

planting.note: A slender branching species with small blue-green rosettes.

directions: Root thin stem cuttings in fast-draining soil and grow in bright, filtered light. Water in the cool growing season and keep drier in summer dormancy. Small white flowers appear on the rosette tips, which die back after blooming.

planting.note: A rounded shrub with broad chalky leaves rimmed in red.

directions: Root stem cuttings in gritty soil and grow in full sun for the whitest leaves and reddest edges. Water deeply but let the soil dry between, keeping it lean in winter. Tall stalks of nodding orange bell flowers rise above the foliage in summer.

planting.note: Fuzzy plump leaves are tipped with reddish claw-like teeth.

directions: Root stem cuttings in fast-draining soil and grow in bright light to keep the toothed leaf tips red. Water at the base when the mix dries, since the hairy leaves drop if kept too wet. Orange bell-shaped flowers hang from short stems in spring.

planting.note: A branching shrublet with fuzzy finger-like leaves tipped in teeth.

directions: Root stem cuttings in gritty soil and grow in bright light for compact growth and reddish leaf tips. Water when the soil dries and keep the fuzzy foliage dry to prevent rot. Orange-yellow bell flowers dangle from upright stems in summer.

planting.note: Grown for fan-shaped silver leaves with wavy crimped edges.

directions: Root stem cuttings in gritty soil and always water at the base to protect the chalky white coating on the leaves. Give bright light and let the mix dry fully between waterings. Tall stems of orange-yellow bell flowers rise above the crimped foliage in summer.

planting.note: A fast, forgiving shrub often used for bonsai and hedging in warm climates.

directions: Push a woody cutting straight into gritty soil, where it roots readily with little fuss. Grow in full sun to bright light and water when the soil dries, tolerating drought once established. Small pink flowers may appear on mature plants in sunny, dry conditions, though it is grown mainly for its jade-like foliage.

planting.note: Set out offsets once nights stay reliably above freezing.

directions: Detach a rooted offset and let the cut surface callus for several days before pressing it into fast-draining mineral soil. Water only when the mix has gone completely dry, keeping it bone dry through winter. Small carmine flowers appear in a ring near the crown in late spring on plants that have been through a cool, dry rest.

planting.note: Sow indoors in warmth during spring for the longest first growing season.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on gritty mix, cover with a dusting of grit, and keep warm and humid until sprouts show. Grow seedlings on with bright light and cautious watering, letting the dense white spine cover develop over several seasons. Pale pink blooms emerge from the woolly crown once the plant reaches a few inches across.

planting.note: Plant divisions after the last frost into a warm, sunny spot.

directions: Twist off a clump section, allow the wound to dry, and root it in coarse cactus mix. Give it strong sun and let the soil dry fully between soakings, withholding water entirely in the cold months. Cream to pale-pink flowers form a neat ring in the dented crown during spring.

planting.note: Move clumps outdoors or up-pot once frost danger has passed.

directions: Separate a dichotomously branching head, dry the cut, and pot it into sharply drained grit. Water sparingly during active growth and keep it dry and cool over winter to set buds. Small yellowish flowers ring the woolly crown in spring above the plant's paired white spines.

planting.note: Start seed in spring warmth; this species tolerates a light frost when dry.

directions: Sprinkle seed over a gritty surface, keep warm and lightly moist until germination, then move to bright light. This flattened, disc-shaped cactus stays low and needs a completely dry winter to survive cold. Cream flowers striped with pink or brown open in a ring near the top in spring.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring for a long first season of growth.

directions: Surface-sow on grit, cover lightly, and keep warm until seedlings appear. This solitary column grows tall over many years and needs a bright, warm position with minimal water. Deep magenta-pink flowers encircle the crown in spring on established plants.

planting.note: Pot up offsets after frost into a bright, warm spot.

directions: Pull away a rooted pup, let it callus, and press it into fast-draining grit. Its soft golden radial spines are easily marked, so water at the base and give strong light to keep the plant tight. Small creamy-white flowers open in a ring around the crown in spring.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring warmth on a gritty surface.

directions: Sow the seed on top of the mix, keep warm and humid, and move seedlings into bright light once established. Grow this cylindrical cactus on with a dry winter rest to encourage flowering. Bright magenta blooms ring the green, glossy crown in spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant offsets after the last frost in a bright location.

directions: Separate a loose, sprawling head, allow it to callus, and root it in coarse cactus mix. The soft green tubercles and spreading spines look almost grass-like, so give full sun and a dry winter to keep it compact. White to cream flowers open near the crown in spring.

planting.note: Set out offsets once frost risk has fully passed.

directions: Detach a rooted pup, dry the wound, and pot it into sharply drained grit. This globose to short-columnar cactus develops stout central spines and needs strong light with sparing water. Small magenta flowers ring the woolly, spiny crown in spring.

planting.note: Plant offsets after frost into rich but fast-draining soil.

directions: Remove a rooted offset, let it callus a few days, and settle it into gritty mix enriched with a little compost. Give more water than most cacti during summer, then keep it cool and dry in winter to trigger flowering. Large fragrant pink trumpets open overnight in late spring and last a day or two.

planting.note: Pot offsets after frost into bright, airy conditions.

directions: Twist off a pup, dry the base, and root it in a fast-draining mix in a spot with bright but slightly filtered light. Water moderately in summer and keep dry through a cool winter rest. Very long-tubed white flowers open at night in summer above the nearly spineless green body.

planting.note: Root the easily-detached finger stems after frost.

directions: Break off one of the finger-like stems, let it dry a day, and lay it on gritty mix where it roots readily. This sprawling, clumping cactus is easy and tolerates cold if kept dry over winter. Bright scarlet-orange flowers cover the plant generously in late spring.

planting.note: Plant offsets after frost in full sun.

directions: Separate a rooted pup, callus the cut, and pot it in gritty soil with a little added compost. Water freely in summer and rest it cool and dry through winter to encourage bloom. Enormous white, sweetly scented trumpets open at night in early summer on long floral tubes.

planting.note: Root columnar cuttings after frost in warm, dry conditions.

directions: Take a length of stem, let the cut end callus for a week or more, then stand it in dry grit until roots form. This branching golden-spined column grows tall and needs full sun with sparing water. Large white nocturnal flowers open near the stem tips in summer.

planting.note: Plant clumping stems after frost in full sun.

directions: Detach a stem from the clump, allow it to callus, and root it in coarse, sharply drained mix. Give full sun and a dry winter rest to support heavy flowering. Showy red to yellow daytime blooms open along the upper stems in early summer.

planting.note: Pot offsets after frost in a bright, warm spot.

directions: Remove a rooted pup, dry the base, and set it into gritty cactus mix. Water moderately during the growing season and keep dry and cool in winter to set buds. Glossy pink funnel-shaped flowers with pale throats open in early summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring; provide light shade from midday sun.

directions: Surface-sow on grit, keep warm and humid, and shade seedlings from harsh sun. This flattened gray-green cactus prefers bright but filtered light and a completely dry winter. White flowers with rose-red throats open near the crown in spring.

planting.note: Start seed in spring; this species takes light frost when dry.

directions: Sow the seed on a gritty surface, keep warm until germination, then grow on in bright, lightly filtered light. This tough dark green cactus is one of the hardier chin cacti if kept dry in winter. White to pale pink flowers open near the top in summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring with light shade for seedlings.

directions: Surface-sow on grit and keep the seedlings warm, humid, and shaded from strong sun. This smooth, glossy green cactus dislikes harsh light and needs a dry winter rest. Large satiny pale-pink to white flowers open near the crown in early summer.

planting.note: Start seed in spring warmth on gritty mix.

directions: Sow on a gritty surface and keep warm and humid until seedlings appear. This large, broad chin cactus grows steadily in bright filtered light with a dry winter rest. Pinkish-white flowers with reddish throats open near the crown in summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring; keep seedlings lightly shaded.

directions: Surface-sow on grit, keep warm, and shade the young plants from midday sun. This ribbed green cactus grows slowly and needs sharp drainage with a completely dry winter. Pale pink to greenish-white flowers open on long tubes near the crown in summer.

planting.note: Pot offsets after frost in a bright, cool position.

directions: Separate a small offset from the clump, let it callus, and root it in gritty mix. This tiny, freely clustering cactus flowers profusely when given a cold, dry winter rest. Funnel-shaped red-orange flowers ring the base of the plant in spring.

planting.note: Pot up offsets after frost in bright light.

directions: Detach a soft white-spined pup, dry the cut, and press it into fast-draining grit. Water at the base to protect the fuzzy spines and keep it dry and cool over winter. Bright orange flowers appear around the plant's base in spring.

planting.note: Plant offsets after frost in a bright, airy spot.

directions: Separate a rooted offset, callus the wound, and root it in gritty cactus mix. The long white bristly spines are decorative, so water carefully and give a cold, dry winter rest. Crimson-red flowers open in a generous ring around the base in spring.

planting.note: Pot offsets after frost in bright light.

directions: Remove a small pup from the cluster, dry it, and root it in sharply drained grit. This densely white-spined little cactus blooms freely after a cold, dry winter. Bright orange flowers open low around the plant in spring.

planting.note: Pot small offsets after frost in a bright spot.

directions: Separate a tiny offset, let it callus, and root it carefully in gritty mix as this species can be slow to establish. Its bodies are covered in neat, comb-like silvery spines and need excellent drainage. Large orange flowers on long tubes open near the base in spring.

planting.note: Plant offsets after frost in full sun.

directions: Detach an offset from the clump, dry the cut, and root it in fast-draining grit. Give strong light and a cold, dry winter to bring on abundant flowers. Golden-yellow blooms, sometimes with red throats, ring the base in spring.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring; shade seedlings from harsh sun.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on grit, keep warm and humid, and shade the young plants. This blue-green ribbed cactus clusters with age and enjoys slightly more water than most cacti in summer. Pale yellow flowers open at the woolly crown in summer.

planting.note: Pot offsets after frost in bright light.

directions: Separate a clustering stem, let it callus, and root it in gritty cactus mix. This soft golden-spined cactus stays globular when young and elongates into a column with age. Lemon-yellow flowers open at the slanted, fuzzy crown once the plant is mature.

planting.note: Pot offsets after frost; shade from hottest sun.

directions: Detach an offset, dry the wound, and root it in a fast-draining mix in bright but filtered light. This flattened, white-spined cactus flowers young and prefers a cool winter rest. Bright red to orange flowers open at the crown in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Plant offsets after frost in bright light.

directions: Remove an offset, allow it to callus, and root it in gritty mix. Its dense white and reddish spines give a silvery look, and it needs sharp drainage with a dry winter. Clear yellow flowers open near the crown in summer.

planting.note: Pot offsets after frost in a bright spot.

directions: Separate one of the freely produced offsets, dry it, and root it in fast-draining mix. This low, glossy green cactus multiplies readily and enjoys moderate summer water with a dry winter. Large glossy yellow flowers open at the crown in summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring warmth on gritty mix.

directions: Surface-sow on grit, keep warm and humid, and grow seedlings on in bright light. This golden-spined globular cactus is slow but rewarding and needs a dry winter to bloom. Small bright yellow flowers cluster at the woolly crown in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring; this barrel takes light frost when dry.

directions: Sow seed on gritty mix, keep warm, and be patient as barrel cacti are slow from seed. Give full sun and very sparing water, keeping the plant bone dry through winter. Yellow flowers open in a ring at the crown once the barrel is well established.

planting.note: Start seed in spring; hardy to light frost when kept dry.

directions: Sow on grit and keep warm until the slow seedlings appear, then grow on in full sun. This barrel has long hooked central spines and needs excellent drainage with a dry winter. Large yellow flowers with red throats open at the crown in summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring warmth on gritty mix.

directions: Sow seed on grit, keep warm and humid, and grow the slow seedlings on in strong light. This barrel is prized for its curving golden to reddish spines and needs sharp drainage and a dry winter. Yellow flowers open in a ring at the crown on mature plants.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring; keep warm for germination.

directions: Sow on gritty mix, keep warm, and grow the slow seedlings in full sun. This barrel develops flattened, hooked red central spines and needs very sharp drainage. Red-and-yellow striped flowers open at the crown once the plant matures.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring; tolerant of coastal cool and light frost when dry.

directions: Sow seed on grit, keep warm until germination, and grow the seedlings on in full sun. This low, broad barrel is native to coastal bluffs and needs excellent drainage with a dry winter. Yellow-green flowers open in a ring at the crown in late spring.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring warmth on gritty mix.

directions: Sow on grit, keep warm and humid, and grow the seedlings in bright light. This smaller barrel has stiff yellowish spines and flowers relatively young for the genus. Lemon-yellow flowers open at the woolly crown in early summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring; provide light shade for seedlings.

directions: Surface-sow the seed on grit, keep warm and humid, and shade seedlings from strong sun. This spineless, white-flecked star-ribbed cactus needs a gritty mix with added limestone and a dry winter. Glossy yellow flowers open at the crown in summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring; shade seedlings from harsh sun.

directions: Surface-sow on a gritty, limey mix, keep warm and humid, and shade the flat seedlings. This spineless disc-shaped cactus is prone to rot, so water sparingly and keep it bone dry in winter. Yellow flowers with red-orange throats open at the crown in spring and summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring warmth on gritty, limey mix.

directions: Surface-sow on grit with added lime, keep warm and humid, and grow seedlings on in bright light. This white-flecked cactus bears long, curling papery spines and needs very sharp drainage. Yellow flowers with red centers open at the crown in summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring; the sturdiest Astrophytum for beginners.

directions: Surface-sow on gritty mix, keep warm, and grow seedlings on in bright light. This ribbed, white-flecked cactus grows into a tall column and is the easiest and fastest of its genus. Pale yellow flowers open at the crown on mature plants in summer.

planting.note: Sow indoors in spring; shade seedlings lightly.

directions: Surface-sow on a gritty, limey mix, keep warm and humid, and shade the young plants. This spineless, densely white-flecked star cactus resembles Bishop's Cap and needs sharp drainage and a dry winter. Yellow flowers with red throats open at the crown in summer.

planting.note: Root segment cuttings in spring or early summer once the parent has finished blooming.

directions: Break off two or three flattened stem segments, let the cut heal a day, then press the base into a gritty, free-draining epiphyte mix. Give bright indirect light and let the top of the soil dry between waterings. To set its scarlet spring flowers, give it a cool, drier rest of about six weeks with long nights through winter.

planting.note: Take cuttings of the bottle-shaped joints in the warm months for quickest rooting.

directions: Set healed jointed cuttings into an airy, fast-draining mix and keep barely moist until new growth appears. Grow in bright filtered light and water regularly through spring and summer, easing off in winter. Small golden-yellow flowers open at the joint tips in late winter to spring after a cool rest.

planting.note: Root the small flattened segments after flowering ends in late spring.

directions: Press a few jointed segments into a loose bark-and-perlite mix and water lightly until rooted. This compact plant likes cool nights, bright indirect light, and steady but modest moisture. Rosy-pink starry flowers appear in spring after a cool, drier winter rest.

planting.note: Root bristly stem tips in warm weather in a hanging container.

directions: Lay or insert healed stem cuttings in a well-drained epiphyte mix and keep lightly humid until rooted. Hang in bright indirect light and water when the mix approaches dryness. Fragrant creamy-white flowers open along the fuzzy stems in autumn, followed by reddish berries.

planting.note: Detach a branching stem cluster and root it during the growing season.

directions: Insert a healed cutting of the clustered pencil-like stems into a gritty, quick-draining medium. Grow in bright filtered light and water moderately, allowing the surface to dry between drinks. Tiny white bell flowers appear at the stem tips in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Root a section of the angular chain-linked stems in the warm months.

directions: Set a healed length of the distinctive winged, chain-like stems into a loose epiphytic mix in a hanging pot. Provide bright indirect light and water when the medium is nearly dry. Small white flowers form along the stem angles, mainly in spring.

planting.note: Root a bundle of the fine thread-like stems in the growing season.

directions: Tuck a few of the slender, densely branching stems into an open, free-draining mix and keep lightly moist until established. Best displayed as a trailer in bright indirect light with even, moderate watering. Tiny greenish-white flowers dot the stems in winter, followed by translucent berries.

planting.note: Root a piece of the densely short-branched stems in warm weather.

directions: Press a healed stem cutting bearing its many short side branches into a gritty epiphyte mix. Grow in bright filtered light and water moderately, letting the top layer dry out first. Small white flowers appear along the short branchlets, followed by white berries.

planting.note: Root one of the flat, paddle-shaped segments in the growing season.

directions: Set a healed flat segment upright in a well-drained bark mix and keep barely moist until roots form. Give bright indirect light and moderate water; the broad chain-linked pads may flush reddish in strong light. Small pale-yellow flowers open along the pad edges in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Root a wavy-edged flattened segment during the warm growing months.

directions: Let a broad, scalloped segment callus, then press it into a loose, fast-draining epiphyte mix. Grow in bright filtered light and water when the surface dries; the wavy pads take on bronze tones in brighter spots. Small cream-colored flowers form along the notched margins in spring.

planting.note: Root a length of the flattened, toothed-edged stems in warm weather.

directions: Insert a healed cutting of the strappy, serrated stems into an airy, free-draining medium in a hanging basket. Provide bright indirect light and steady moderate watering during growth. Bell-shaped cream flowers with a yellow throat hang along the margins in spring, followed by red berries.

planting.note: Root a section of the fine cylindrical stems in the growing season.

directions: Root a healed cutting of the slender, freely branching stems in a gritty epiphyte mix. Grow as a trailer in bright indirect light with even, moderate watering. Small white flowers appear at the stem tips, followed by white berries in winter.

planting.note: Root a cylindrical stem section during the warm months.

directions: Set a healed length of the rounded, jointed stems into a well-drained bark-based mix. Grow in bright filtered light and water when the medium nears dryness. Small creamy flowers open at the joints and give way to white, woolly-based berries.

planting.note: Root a piece of the angled, branching stems in the growing season.

directions: Insert a healed cutting of the ribbed, pendent stems into a loose, quick-draining epiphyte mix. Hang in bright indirect light and water moderately through the growing season. Tiny pale flowers form along the stems, followed by small pale berries.

planting.note: Root a section of the zigzag flattened stem in spring or summer.

directions: Allow a cut length of the deeply lobed, zigzag stem to callus for a day or two, then set it in a chunky, free-draining mix. Grow in bright indirect light and water when the top inch dries, easing back in winter. Large, fragrant white flowers open at night in autumn on mature plants.

planting.note: Root a flat stem segment during the warm growing season.

directions: Let a broad flattened cutting callus, then anchor it in a coarse, well-drained epiphyte mix. Provide bright filtered light and regular water in the growing season, reducing it in winter. Large white night-blooming flowers open on established plants in summer.

planting.note: Root a section of the stiff, scalloped-edged flat stems in warm weather.

directions: Callus a flattened cutting for a day, then set it in a chunky bark-based mix and water sparingly until rooted. Grow in bright indirect light with even moisture through the growing season. Large creamy-white, fragrant flowers open in late spring and are widely used to breed hybrid orchid cacti.

planting.note: Root a length of the long flattened stem during the warm months.

directions: Root a healed flat-stemmed cutting in a coarse, free-draining epiphyte mix and give it something to trail from or scramble over. Grow in bright indirect light with regular watering in summer and a drier rest in winter. Slender, long-tubed white flowers open at night in the warm season.

planting.note: Root a flattened stem cutting bearing its bristly areoles in warm weather.

directions: Callus a broad stem cutting, then plant it in a coarse, quick-draining mix and keep barely moist until rooted. Grow in bright filtered light with regular summer water and a cooler, drier winter. Large creamy night-opening flowers with prominent stamens appear on mature plants in early summer.

planting.note: Root a flat, toothed stem segment in spring or summer.

directions: Let a flattened, notched cutting callus, then set it in a coarse epiphyte mix and water lightly until rooted. Grow in bright indirect light with even moisture in the growing season and a slight winter rest. Large, showy red day-blooming flowers open in spring and early summer.

planting.note: Root a length of the slender trailing stem in the warm months.

directions: Callus a piece of the thin, ribbed trailing stem, then insert the base into a gritty, free-draining mix in a hanging pot. Give bright light, even bright direct morning sun, and water regularly in spring and summer with a drier winter rest. Bright pink to magenta flowers line the stems in spring.

planting.note: Root a flattened stem segment during the growing season.

directions: Let a flat leaf-like cutting callus, then plant it in a coarse, well-drained epiphyte mix. Grow in bright indirect light with steady moderate watering in the warm season. Clusters of pink day-blooming flowers open in spring; this species is a classic parent of many orchid-cactus hybrids.

planting.note: Root a section of the slender trailing stem in warm weather.

directions: Callus a piece of the flat, notched trailing stem and set it into a coarse, quick-draining mix in a hanging container. Grow in bright indirect light and water regularly through spring and summer, easing off in winter. Trumpet-shaped rose-red flowers hang from the stem edges in spring and early summer.

planting.note: Root a length of the pendent flattened stems in the growing season.

directions: Insert a healed cutting of the strappy, trailing stems into an airy, free-draining epiphyte mix in a hanging basket. Grow in bright indirect light with even, moderate watering during active growth. Small white to pale flowers form along the stem margins, followed by pale berries.

planting.note: Sow in autumn or spring onto a gritty mineral mix and keep barely moist until the paired bodies form.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on lean, fast-draining grit and mist lightly, keeping the tray bright but out of scorching midday sun. Water only during the autumn-to-spring growing season and withhold entirely in summer while the old skins dry to a papery sheath. Expect the bright yellow, sweetly scented flowers to open in late autumn once plants are a few years old.

planting.note: Start seed in late summer to early autumn so seedlings establish before the cool growing months.

directions: Press seed onto a firmed, quartz-rich mineral mix and keep humid until the smooth spherical bodies appear. Grow hard with strong light and sparse water so the marble-white heads stay firm rather than stretched. Small musky-scented orange flowers push through the tip in autumn on mature clumps.

planting.note: Sow thinly in autumn and shade lightly until the tiny bodies harden off.

directions: Scatter the dust-fine seed over gritty compost and cover the tray to hold moisture during germination. As clumps of small rounded heads build up, water only in the cooler active season and keep bone dry through summer dormancy. Pink to magenta daisy-like flowers appear in autumn on established mats.

planting.note: Best sown in autumn to match the plant's cool-season growth rhythm.

directions: Surface-sow onto sharp mineral grit and keep just moist and bright until the flat-topped, speckled bodies form. Resume watering as summer heat fades, allowing the papery old sheaths to protect the new heads. Look for small nocturnal, sweetly fragrant flowers opening at the fissure in autumn.

planting.note: Sow in autumn so young plants grow through the cool season and rest in summer.

directions: Sprinkle seed over firmed gritty mix and keep covered and humid until the small grape-like bodies emerge. Grow in strong light with restrained watering to build tight, dark-dotted clumps rather than soft stretched heads. Dark purple to magenta flowers open in the evenings of autumn on mature colonies.

planting.note: Autumn sowing suits this cool-growing cone plant.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed on mineral grit and hold humidity until the kidney-shaped bodies with their red-lipped fissures develop. Water only during the autumn and winter growth phase, keeping plants dry and bright in summer. The small pale flowers appear at the top slit in autumn once clumps mature.

planting.note: Sow in autumn to align with the plant's winter-growing habit.

directions: Scatter seed on gritty compost and keep bright and lightly moist until the flat, dot-flecked bodies form. Give ample light and lean watering during the cool months, then withhold water entirely through summer rest. Fragrant cream-colored night-opening flowers rise from the fissure in autumn.

planting.note: Sow in spring onto a mineral mix and shade seedlings from harsh sun their first season.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on gritty, low-organic soil and keep it humid and warm until the paired olive-green bodies emerge. Water sparingly in spring and autumn and stop completely while the plant absorbs its old leaves and reforms new ones. Yellow daisy-like flowers open from the central fissure in autumn on mature plants.

planting.note: Spring sowing gives the longest first growing season before the summer rest.

directions: Sow onto firmed mineral grit and hold gentle humidity until the translucent green bodies form and cluster. Grow bright and dry, watering only in the cooler active seasons so the heads stay plump but compact. Bright yellow flowers appear at the fissure in autumn on established clumps.

planting.note: Sow in spring and protect seedlings from strong direct sun early on.

directions: Surface-sow on sharp mineral mix and keep just moist until the flat-topped, network-patterned bodies appear. Give bright light and very restrained water, letting the pot dry fully between the few waterings in spring and autumn. White flowers open from the central slit in autumn once plants reach flowering size.

planting.note: Spring sowing lets seedlings bulk up before their first summer dormancy.

directions: Scatter fine seed over gritty compost and keep warm and humid until the buff, red-dotted bodies emerge. Water only lightly during active growth and keep the plant completely dry while it exchanges old leaves for new. Yellow flowers open at the fissure in autumn on mature plants.

planting.note: Sow in spring and grow bright but shaded from midday scorch the first year.

directions: Surface-sow onto mineral grit and cover to maintain humidity until the paired bodies with their distinctive marginal windows appear. Water only in spring and autumn, keeping plants dry through the summer and mid-winter rests. Yellow flowers emerge from the central fissure in autumn on established plants.

planting.note: Sow in spring to give the longest establishment window before dormancy.

directions: Sow the fine seed on firmed grit and keep humid and warm until the greyish, faintly patterned bodies form. Grow with strong light and minimal water so the heads stay firm and flush with the soil surface. White flowers open at the fissure in autumn on flowering-size plants.

planting.note: Spring sowing works best for this warm-germinating living stone.

directions: Surface-sow onto a gritty mineral mix and keep humid until the reddish, dark-flecked bodies emerge. Water sparingly during the spring and autumn growth periods and keep bone dry through the summer rest. Yellow flowers open from the fissure in autumn once the plant reaches maturity.

planting.note: Sow in spring and shade seedlings lightly through their first summer.

directions: Scatter seed over sharp grit and keep just moist and warm until the brownish, marbled bodies appear. Give bright light and infrequent water during active growth, withholding entirely while the old leaves are reabsorbed. White flowers open at the central slit in autumn on established plants.

planting.note: Best sown in spring or autumn and grown hard in full sun.

directions: Sow onto gritty compost and keep just damp until the thick, grey-green rock-like leaves form. Water sparingly and only when thoroughly dry, since these succulents are extremely prone to bursting or rotting if overfed with moisture. Golden-orange flowers open in the afternoon of autumn on plants a few years old.

planting.note: Sow in autumn or spring in a mineral-rich, limestone-friendly grit.

directions: Surface-sow onto a coarse, alkaline-leaning grit and keep just moist until the warty, encrusted rosettes form. Grow this winter-active mesemb bright and dry, watering only in the cool season when the plant is in growth. Small golden-yellow flowers open in late winter to spring on established rosettes.

planting.note: Sow in autumn to suit this winter-growing tuberous mesemb.

directions: Sow onto deep gritty soil so the swelling taproot has room, keeping the surface just moist until small warty rosettes appear. Water during the cooler active months and rest the plant dry in summer heat. Coppery-yellow flowers open in the afternoon during the cool season on mature plants.

planting.note: Sow in autumn or spring in a lean, fast-draining mix and avoid rich soil.

directions: Sow onto gritty soil and keep just moist until the soft, strap-shaped green tongues emerge in two ranks. Keep the mix very lean and water sparingly, as rich soil and excess water make the leaves grow flabby and shapeless. Large glossy-yellow flowers open in autumn and winter on established clumps.

planting.note: Sow in autumn to match this winter-growing mesemb's rhythm.

directions: Surface-sow onto gritty compost and keep just moist until the paired, claw-like grey-green leaves develop. Water during the cool growing season and keep dry in summer while the old leaf pair dries to a protective sheath. Large pale yellow to cream flowers open in late winter to spring on mature plants.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring or summer in a gritty succulent mix.

directions: Take short stem cuttings, let the cut end callus for a day, then insert into fast-draining gritty soil to root. Grow this bushy species in bright light with sparing water, letting the soil dry fully between drinks. Small white to pale yellow flowers appear intermittently through the warm season along the bristly stems.

common_name: Jewel of Desert Moonstone Ice Plant

planting.note: Set out nursery plants after the last spring frost into sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant transplants at soil level in full sun and lean, gritty, well-drained ground where water never pools. Water lightly to establish, then let this hardy mat go nearly dry between rains, as good winter drainage is key to survival. Creamy-white flowers with pale centers cover the mat from early to midsummer.

common_name: Jewel of Desert Topaz Ice Plant

planting.note: Transplant after frost into full sun and gritty, fast-draining soil.

directions: Set plants level with the surrounding soil in a hot, sunny bed with excellent drainage. Keep water minimal once established and avoid heavy mulch over the crown, which can trap moisture and cause rot. Bicolor blooms in soft yellow and pink smother the low mat through early and midsummer.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost into full sun with very sharp drainage.

directions: Set transplants at grade in a lean, gritty, sun-baked bed and water just enough to root them in. This vigorous large-flowered selection asks for little water once settled and resents wet feet in winter. Big orange flowers with lighter centers blanket the spreading mat from late spring through summer.

scientific_name: Delosperma 'Wheels of Wonder Hot Pink Wonder'

planting.note: Transplant after frost into a hot, sunny, sharply drained spot.

directions: Plant at soil level in gritty, well-drained ground and give full sun for the heaviest flowering. Water sparingly after establishment and ensure the crown stays dry over winter to prevent rot. Large hot-pink flowers with pale eyes cover the low spreading mat from late spring into summer.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring or summer in a gritty succulent mix.

directions: Let stem cuttings callus briefly, then root them in fast-draining gritty soil in bright light. Grow this stacked, angular-leaved species on the dry side, watering only when the soil is fully dry. Small yellow flowers open in the warm season on established, sprawling stems.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring or plant nursery stock after frost in a sunny, dry bed.

directions: Root firm stem cuttings in gritty soil after a short callus period, then grow in full sun with lean water. Prune lightly after bloom to keep the low shrub dense and floriferous. Brilliant scarlet-red daisy flowers cover the plant in a dazzling spring display.

planting.note: Take cuttings in spring or set out plants after frost in a hot, dry spot.

directions: Root stem cuttings in sharp, gritty soil and grow in full sun with minimal irrigation. Shear lightly after flowering to maintain a tidy mounded habit. Masses of glowing golden-orange daisy flowers blanket the shrub in spring.

planting.note: Root cuttings in autumn or spring in gritty soil for this cool-season grower.

directions: Root stem cuttings in fast-draining grit and grow in full sun to keep the finger-like blue-green leaves compact. Water during the cool active season and keep drier in summer heat. Vivid carmine-red flowers with contrasting stamens open in the afternoon during late winter and spring.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring and plant in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Insert callused stem cuttings into gritty soil and grow this tough groundcover in full sun with little water. It tolerates heat, poor soil, and coastal exposure once established. Coppery-orange to salmon flowers open on and off through the warm season, peaking in spring.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring and set out on sunny, well-drained banks.

directions: Root the bristly, sparkling-leaved stems in gritty soil and let them sprawl into a dense groundcover. Grow in full sun with minimal water once established, as it thrives on neglect in dry sites. Bright magenta-purple flowers cover the mat profusely in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Root cuttings in spring in gritty soil; raise the tuberous roots for bonsai effect.

directions: Root stem cuttings or grow from seed in gritty soil, gradually exposing the thickened roots to create a bonsai-like caudex. Grow in bright light with sparing water, letting the soil dry between drinks. Bright magenta daisy flowers open above the bristle-tipped leaves during the warm season.

planting.note: Root cuttings or set tubers in warm indoor conditions once nights stay reliably mild.

directions: Lay a trimmed strand on gritty, fast-draining mix and pin the nodes lightly to the surface so aerial tubers can root. Give bright, filtered light and water only when the mix dries fully, easing off in winter. Small tubular magenta blooms appear along mature vines in late summer.

planting.note: Propagate from tuber-bearing cuttings in warm, bright conditions after frost has passed.

directions: Press node segments onto a lean, sharp-draining medium and keep barely moist until roots take hold. The cream-and-pink leaf margins color best under strong indirect light, so avoid deep shade that fades the variegation. Water sparingly and expect the same lantern-shaped blooms as the plain form once vines lengthen.

planting.note: Start stem cuttings in warm weather and provide a trellis for the twining shoots.

directions: Insert cuttings into gritty mix and support the slender vines as they climb. Bright, filtered light and thorough-but-infrequent watering suit it best. The green, parachute-shaped flowers with fused canopy tips signal a happy, established plant in the warm season.

planting.note: Lay cuttings of the mottled snake-like stems on warm, dry mix to root.

directions: Set a segment of the thick, camouflage-patterned stem flat on sharp-draining soil and keep it dry until rooted. Grow in bright indirect light and water only after the soil dries completely. Spotted, tubular flowers rise on wiry stalks once the plant is settled.

planting.note: Root leafless green stems in warm conditions and give them something to twine on.

directions: Push cuttings of the near-leafless stems into gritty mix and support them as they climb. Provide strong filtered light and let the soil dry thoroughly between waterings. The showy white-and-green striped, bulb-based flowers open in the warm months on a well-grown vine.

planting.note: Root strand cuttings in warm, bright indoor conditions.

directions: Trail cuttings of the narrow, needle-like leaves across lean mix and anchor the nodes to root. Bright indirect light keeps the fine foliage dense, and watering should wait until the mix is dry. Slim magenta lantern flowers appear along established stems in summer.

planting.note: Root twining stem cuttings in warm weather and give them a support.

directions: Set cuttings in fast-draining mix and let the thin stems climb a trellis or wire. Grow in bright filtered light and water only when the soil has dried. Curious speckled flowers, topped by a dark hair-tipped knob on a stalk, form on mature vines.

planting.note: Root cuttings on a bark slab or in an airy epiphyte mix during warm weather.

directions: Pin cuttings to a loose, bark-based or sphagnum medium since this is a climbing epiphyte rather than a soil plant. Keep it warm and humid in bright indirect light, allowing the surface to dry slightly between waterings. Rows of round, coin-like leaves trail down and produce tiny cream flowers when mature.
